sentences.text: It's hard to tell.
It was in the height of the winter.
No problem.
Go ahead.
Came to us in the height of the winter of 1942.
You asked me who took care of the people during the summer of '42.
So you had my three brothers, Tuvia, Asael, and Zisl.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] Again, it's hard for me-- I'll do it to the best of my ability.
It's going to be hard for you to believe-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] hard to comprehend, even, never mind believing.
If you were in the company of-- of those three brothers-- [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] you felt like you have a whole army behind you.
You were fearless.
You were not afraid of anybody if they were in your-- if you were in their company, you are fearless.
They were-- Tuvia and Zisl were over 6 feet tall.
They were very powerful men.
Asael was somewhat shorter, 5' 8' or 5' 9', but I think strength-wise was probably-- Tuvia and Asael strengthwise, they were very healthy.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] They were down-to-earth people-- farmers, which this is-- I mean, I'm giving you my personal opinion.
I don't know if you'll agree or not.

[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] There is a different-- when a person grows up in the city in luxury or a person grows up on a farm, and from his childhood, he had to take care of himself because he was-- because his religion is Jewish-- this is my own interpretation.
[PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] [BACKGROUND NOISE] I'll tell you-- give you an example what my brothers were.
I'll give you an example on one-- Zisl Alexander.
[PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] There was a man in Israel that he was bending steel.
There are.
Those are people, very few in a country like America, not only Israel.
For some reason or another, he went in on a turf fight with my brother Zisl.
My brother Zisl grabbed a baseball bat.
And he said, your steel is not going to help you.
And he gave in-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] to my brother.
I'm giving you an example what kind of a man-- you asked me what kind of a man they-- they were.
Zisl was a hot-head.
He would-- hit first and ask questions later.
Tuvia was a much more conservative-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] person.
He would think twice before to hurt anyone.
[PAUSES FOR 14 SECONDS] But the most unique man-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I would say had to be Asael.
[PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] I don't know why it worked out how it worked out, but he was the-- the main man in the family, even as far as I can remember to 1940, 1939, '38.
Below that, I wouldn't remember anything.
He was the main man in the family.
He was a man without a heart, a very good person.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] And my personal opinion is that-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] he-- he was the power.
He was the strength of those three brothers.
Zisl and him was like twins.
Asael was somewhat older, about a year and a half from Zisl.
But Zisl would listen to him.
No one in the family would ever contradict Asael.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And for some reason or another, the Gentile world knew there is more brothers than Asael and Zisl.
But if you will talk to them, Tuvia is older.
He would come later on.
The main two that they were afraid of was Asael and Zisl.
So you ask me what happened of summer 1942.
Am I correct?
Yes.
You were still 60 people then?
We're about 50, 60 people.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS]
So then the only thing was to-- my brothers had arms.
But they were the only ones.
The rest of them came out with nothing.
War is a problem.
To hide, there is no problem because the woods are big.
But to get food, you have to go at night to some farmers on good terms, to some on bad.
You can't always come to a-- to five farmers and they should support you constantly.
They don't have-- they're not that wealthy.

[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And particularly to the good friends, to the good farmers that helped us all along and give us information constantly, that our parents were butchered, together with other 5,000 Jews in-- in [INAUDIBLE], you don't want to go to him and ask him for food for 50 people.
Was a-- was a problem simply to feed.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Which some older Jews from-- my sister is from Toby's family.
Her father-in-law and her brother-in-laws, made a remark that there was no more, that's even too many already we are.
And then, on those words, came out Tuvia.
He was the first one that said, if there is a Jewish child, if there is a Jewish woman or an old Jew, if he wants to come to us, whatever we will eat, he will have.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And word got around in the ghettos-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] that Tuvia has said that.
And this is the way-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] he got very popular among-- among the weaker Jewish people.
Yes.
They would not do it or if not him.
They would have never done that-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] without him.
Zisl would-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] would not let-- [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] would not go after this idea because it is a responsibility to get together that many people and take care of them.
I don't think-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Asael have seen that far the way Tuvia did.
I do not think so.
He made simply a remark-- let any Jewish child, so long it is Jewish-- old man, sick man-- if they Jewish, they have a place to come.
I'll give you an example.
There came a small group of people, maybe 10 or 12.
Among them were two or three women.

Comes one young man to my brothers, to those three brothers, and said, another so-called-- so-called female came that you can go-- that anybody could go to-- to bed with-- in other words, a call girl.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]
Who the hell needs them here?
He made a remark, a Jewish young man.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]
So Tuvia told him-- and the-- and the others heard, other women and older men heard what he said.
He said, you ever say that, and your life will end on the spot.
Don't ever call a Jewish woman-- [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] a call girl.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] And from there and on, to those people, he was like God.
And he was good to them.
He was a good-hearted individual.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And he was capable.
Not everybody could take a Gentile identification and appear as a Gentile man, being real Jewish, in a Gentile world.
It's not so easy.
The biggest group would be 10, 15.
They could not.
Same as anybody else.
What you mean how--
Well, so we had about 50, 60 people by then.
How do you go about to get food?
And they had no arms at all the first summer.
So they would take a long stick, longer than a baseball bat.
[POINTS] This is a true story, by the way, what I'm telling you.
[LAUGHS] We'd take a long stick, right, and take a cord, put on one side of the stick and on the other side so it looks like a model of a rifle, right?
Do I make myself clear?
And then you put on the shoulders.
It's look-- looks like at night.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] At night, if you-- in the house on a farm, in the house, it's light.
Outside is dark.
So let's say, if you look at person walks with a-- the way I described, the-- that stick with a cord, and he has it on his shoulders, it looks like a rifle.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]
So word got around that the Bielski brothers have a-- a whole army behind them.
And they maybe took at a time, this episode, maybe 15 or 20 men with sticks, should look like rifles.
And the villagers should see at night, those are armed people.
And in no-- in no time, word got around that the Bielski brothers have a whole army behind them.
So what would they do?
They would come into a farmhouse?
Come into a farm house and-- and-- and tell them to give them a loaf of bread or milk, whatever they had.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And then, slowly, the very same summer, they started to get arms, by all means, by any means possible.
Well, we had Gentile-- as I said, some of our Gentile people that were good people and knew my brothers, my brothers knew them well, through them, they bought.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] They helped them.
They gave them.
And when they got a couple of rifles or a couple of machine guns, then already was no problem.
Because then they would go and see by force where to catch a policeman or a few policemen or a few Germans and take away their arms-- butcher them, kill them, and they would have arms.
And this is the way it started.
[DRINKS]
Well, towards the end of '42, we had already quite a few hundred people.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And the way my brothers knew a certain area of people, a certain amount of Gentile, so did other Jews knew where is a wealth area, where is a poorer area.
And they already knew who is on our side and who is on the German side.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And that's the way they would go at night and get as far as food goes.
But this was not the main-- this was the main problem to exist.
But the main problem to my brothers I guess, was at the time to repay those collaborators that gave us-- my parents-- terrorized us in the beginning of the war.
And that fellow that he told the Nazis they should arrest me-- if you remember that Polish man-- that I was arrested for a few hours.
First of all, what they did is paid them with interest.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] This very same fellow, that he arrested me with the Nazis in Nowogrodek in 1943.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] In 1943, again, it was in the winter.
Among the good Gentile people that were on our side got word to my brothers that he came from the city to his girlfriend, and he sleeps with her in this-and-this village, this-and-this farm.
When a dozen people-- they arrested him.
They brought him into the woods.
No, they did not bring him to the woods.
They-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] They went to burn a bridge at a time.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] They took him to the bridge.
One of the men, his name was-- I'll even mention his name.
Let him rest in peace.
His name was Gulkovich.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] Arrested him.
He was crying when he saw my brothers.
[PAUSES FOR 9 SECONDS]
A ax, and chopped his head off.
Did not tortures him.
Chopped his head off.
One of our men, Gulkovich.
Correct, of course.
Threw him in the burned bridge beyond recognition.
And they put a sign on.
This is going to be here and here, got killed So-and-so-- his name.
The reason-- for collaborating with the Nazis.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] And obvious, word gets around very quickly.
Then this one that worked for us-- that's the shop that I said-- his son became also a policeman.
And he was looking for my brothers with Nazis.
My brothers caught him-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] in 1943, brought in to the woods again.
He was crying when he saw them.
And he hung-- they-- the partisans hung him in the woods.
Did not burn him like the other.
So this-- this was the first that my brothers did, as far as I'm concerned.
They repaid.
And in other words, when you do that, the next Gentile should know, maybe you could become more civilized-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] and leave the Jews alone as much as possible.
Did that work?
For some of them, did.
Some of them-- [LAUGHS]-- continued.
But the fact remains we survived.
[DRINKS] When we lived through the winter from 1942 to '43-- [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] they were already pretty powerful and knowledgeable.
If you can live a year, why couldn't you live another year?
Why couldn't you live another 10 years?
If that's supposed to be, then that's what it is.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]
So then my brothers would send to the ghetto people-- their relatives, whatever they could, whatever was left over that the Nazis did not killed.
They all came out.
Then they started to take out from the ghettos people that we knew.
And then people came along just because they're Jewish, because word by word, word by mouth-- by mouth got around.
And this is the way it started to grow to way over 1,000 people.
We were chased from one area into the other.
Sometimes the Nazis would surprise us.
Well, we were in one area called [NON-ENGLISH], and, again, in the height of the winter, that's where my brother Tuvia lost his wife.
We lost at that time about 10, 12 people.
They surprised us, the Nazis.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]
But the rest of us survived.
Would you like to tell us about it?
What happened?
Where?
How?
Well, what happened, it was in the height of the winter.
And we were in  a certain part of the woods.
State of White Russia, everything in the very same--
No.
Really, that's--
Not that specific region.
The big woods-- the endless woods-- they had a name, which later on we went.
But in the beginning, we were around our village, around our area, because we knew every crack in the ground.
It was in the height of the winter.
We were already a few hundred people.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And the Nazis surprised us.
Apparently, some-- somehow it got to them that here is a bunch of Jews, or the Bielskis.
And they killed about 10, 12 people.
Among them was my brother's-- Tuvia's wife.
This was the biggest loss we had in one shot during course of the war.
About 10 or 12 people were lost.
All of them?
No, my brother's wife?
Sonya.
Oh, yeah, a relative of ours-- [?
Grisha. ?] [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]
Handsome as they come-- tall, 6-foot something.
How was he related to you?
How was he related to-- [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] He was actually through Tuvia's family, through his marriage to Sonya.
I couldn't tell you exactly.
I couldn't remember no names, who else.
[INAUDIBLE].
Me, personally?
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] I was with one group of people.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I think with-- the only one I could remember that was with me is one woman.
She still lives in New Jersey.
Sula ?] [?
Wolkowicki. ?]
Oh, yeah.
We were in-- was in the raid.
What do you see if-- [LAUGHS] you see Nazi soldiers shooting at you-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] a barrage of bullets coming at you.
50 yards away.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] 50 yards away, the closest.
If they would be closer, be bad news.
I don't know, but a lot.
I would say like a few hundred.
Nothing.
They killed a few.
The rest, we ran away.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And we did not go anymore to that specific end of the woods, in this particular part.
Of course they came after us.
But then they will lose us.
They were somewhat afraid, too.
I don't know.
I couldn't answer that.
At this particular incident, I don't know.
Anywhere, so long as the woods-- the other way.
If you see them over there, you run there-- simple.
You don't need no degree for that-- instinct.
Again, sure, after a few hours, I guess, or late at night.
We went to a different part of the area.
Left this area altogether.
Went to an area like 20 kilometers away.
You see, when I say 20 kilometers, 10 miles, it's not like the distance over here 10 miles.
The distance is the same.
But over there, 10 miles, it would be like here 200.
How is that?
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]
I understood.
